Volunteer Opportunities

- ## Live Performances & Talks
	Experience the thrill of live jazz and help create an unforgettable event! As a volunteer, you'll assist with event setup, manage the flow of guests, and support the performers. This is your chance to be at the heart of the action, enjoy amazing music, and contribute to a memorable experience for everyone. ***Join us and be a part of the rhythm!***
- ## Jazz Lab Mobile
	Jazz Lab Mobile brings the magic of jazz, science, and creativity together to spark curiosity in young minds. Through hands-on activities that explore sound, vibrations, and musical expression, students develop critical 21st-century skills while thinking creatively and discovering new ideas. If you're patient, a good listener, and passionate about working with youth, we’d love to have you on our team. **Perfect for retirees, former educators, at-home mothers, and college students!**
- ## Jazz Lab Summer
	Jazz Lab Summer is an exciting 6-days of hands-on, immersive STEAM learning! From VR to coding, to drumming and Ai world-building - ten dynamic labs under one roof, giving youth of all ages an opportunity to try new cultural and technological experiences!!
	**Summer dates: June 16-18 and June 23-25 from 9-1:30 PM!**"

Jazzmobile's mission is to present, preserve, promote and propagate America's classical music, Jazz.  
  
Founded in 1964 by National Endowment for the Arts Jazz Master, pianist, Dr. Billy Taylor and philanthropist and arts administrator, Ms. Daphne Anstein, Jazzmobile is the first U.S. not-for-profit arts and cultural organization created just for Jazz. The Herbie Hancock Institute of Jazz is a nonprofit education organization with a mission to offer the world’s most promising young musicians college level training by internationally acclaimed jazz masters and to present public school music education programs for young people around the world. The Institute preserves, perpetuates and expands jazz as a global art form, and utilizes jazz as a means to unite people of all ages, backgrounds and nationalities. All of the Institute’s programs are provided free of charge to students, schools and communities worldwide. The Institute’s programs use jazz as the medium to encourage imaginative thinking, creativity, a positive self-image, and a respect for one’s own and others’ cultural heritage.

Founded in 1986 as the Thelonious Monk Institute of Jazz, the organization began operating as the Herbie Hancock Institute of Jazz in January 2019 in recognition of Mr. Hancock’s commitment...[truncated 11491 chars]...“A White House Jazz Festival,” the first “In Performance at The White House” PBS special taped with President and Mrs. Clinton. In 1996, the Institute produced “A Celebration of America’s Music,” the first network television special devoted to jazz in over 25 years, which aired on ABC. A second “A Celebration of America’s Music” aired in 1998. In 2006, President and Mrs. Bush hosted a concert celebrating the Institute’s 20th anniversary that aired as an “In Performance at The White House” PBS special hosted by Barbara Walters. In addition, the Institute’s international jazz competitions have been featured as documentaries on Black Entertainment Television and its affiliates. Recent productions have included the 2016 ABC special “Jazz at the White House,” hosted by President and Mrs. Obama, and “ [International Jazz Day 10th Anniversary Celebration](https://www.pbs.org/show/international-jazz-day/),” a national television event that aired on PBS in honor of International Jazz Day 2021."

JOI Mission

JOI Vision

JOI was founded to fill a need in the community for greater exposure to jazz studies and awareness, focusing on students of all ages. Through festivals, clinics, “Band Directors Academy,” "Jazz for Young People Concerts", and other outreach programs, JOI celebrates the great American art form by promoting it through the next generation of performers, audiences, and consumers.

[Donate](/)

[Subscribe](/)

JOI was founded by Las Vegas native Kenny Rampton who is currently a member of The Jazz at Lincoln Center Orchestra. JOI draws inspiration from The Jazz at Lincoln Center Orchestra Artistic Director, Wynton Marsalis.

"Jazz is a metaphor for Democracy. Because jazz is improvisational, it celebrates personal freedom and encourages individual expression. Because jazz is swinging, it dedicates that freedom to finding and maintaining common ground with others. Because jazz is rooted in the blues, it inspires us to face adversity with persistent optimism."

The New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Foundation’s Community Outreach Tickets program provides entry to the New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival presented by Shell (Jazz Fest), the Foundation’s main fundraising event.

The Community Outreach Ticket program is for Louisiana-based non-profits that provide **DIRECT social services.**

Social services are defined as:  
“Benefits and facilities such as education, food subsidies, health care, and subsidized housing provided by a non-profit organization to improve the life and living conditions of the children, the disabled, the elderly, and the poor in the national community.” The purpose of this program is to provide access to Jazz Fest for those who otherwise could not afford to attend the festival.

Eligible organizations must provide these services directly to individuals on a regular, ongoing basis (daily, weekly, monthly). Agencies that provide only occasional services are ...[truncated 1316 chars]...in need. These organizations aim to promote the welfare and well-being of individuals, families, and communities by providing services that address a wide range of social issues such as poverty, homelessness, health care, mental health, education, child welfare, and senior services.”

To be eligible for Community Outreach Tickets, your organization must provide these services directly to individuals on a regular, ongoing basis (daily, weekly, monthly). Agencies that provide only occasional services are not eligible. Agencies that provide services only to groups (such as classes) are not eligible.

## Why Jazz?

Below are three core reasons that reveal how jazz can be the most dynamic music genre to transform students’ lives.

## 1\. Jazz Speaks for Life

As the great Martin Luther King Jr. said in his opening address at the 1964 Berlin Jazz Festival, "Jazz Speaks for Life". Jazz was formed by great Americans that grew up in poverty such as Louis Armstrong and Billie Holiday. These pioneers did not only overcome their personal poverty through jazz, they also helped found America’s most prized cultural contribution to the world. The solidarity that today’s low income youth can find with the pioneers of jazz has the potential not only to change the course of their own lives, but also the future of jazz.

## 2\. The Practicality of Jazz Programs

Low income schools usually have very limited funds, especially when it comes to funding the arts. Running a jazz program at a school is significantly less expensive than other instrumental music programs because there are really only two main costs: instruments and sheet music. The flexibility of the size of a jazz band allows both smaller and larger schools to develop strong jazz programs.

## 3\. The Rigor of Jazz

Due to its heavy focus on ear training, music theory, and improvisation, jazz music is one of the most rigorous styles of music to play. This can help maximize all of the cognitive benefits that come from learning music."

## Program Overview

We currently offer 3 programs to provide as much accessibility as possible to Long Beach, Orange County, and greater Los Angeles area. Our most popular program is in-person learning at our Signal Hill studio location, offering classes Sunday’s, Monday’s, and Tuesday’s. For the homeschooled youth of Southern California we are approved vendors for a number of charter homeschool programs. Through this program these children can begin to build lifetime friendships with other dedicated musicians. Lastly Jazz Angels offers in-school programs within the greater Los Angeles area. Schools will have access to once-weekly jazz lessons as part of their regular music class curriculum. We welcome participation from all local schools/districts who are interested!

### Studio Semester Sessions

The Jazz Angels’ Studio Programs currently serve about 70 young musicians at a time, divided into 6-8 bands based on abil...[truncated 1348 chars]...tudents additional exposure to improvisational jazz while allowing their regular music teacher the freedom to circulate around the room to observe and give individual attention to students as needed.

We welcome participation from all local schools/districts who are interested.

### Jazz Legacy Program

Jazz Angels established the Jazz Legacy Program (JLP) to introduce our young musicians to the giants of jazz – famous jazz artists whose pioneering work shaped the jazz music we know today. Via this program, our young musicians learn about their music, lives and contributions that these legendary artists made both within and outside of jazz.

We create special arrangements of their most famous songs, including solos diretly transcribed from thier recordings. Our mentors guide the student musicians to learn, rehearse and perform the music. In so doing, our musicians go beyond standard education and actually experience the legends’ jazz, especially when playing transcribed solos!"

"<webfetch-citation source_id="7h6t-D8rxjVAwBu7qkQqU" />

## Volunteer

## Keep the Jazz community strong!!

Are you an accomplished jazz musician who is looking for ways to share your knowledge and skills with the youth? Maybe you just love jazz music and would like to help out the local community. Either way Jazz Angels is always looking for new volunteers to help our organization enrich the community. We have a number of ways that we use volunteers in our programs from helping teach studio sessions to working one-on-one with with the students as a student mentor, all the way to helping. Please reach out to our team today to begin the discussion of how you would like to be involved as well as explain your musical background.

## In-Studio Volunteer

Over the years the strength of the Jazz Angels program has been largely impacted by the amazing volunteers in and around Long Beach and Orange County. We have a number of different ways for people to get involved with helping our organization grow and thrive. We are always looking for talented jazz musicians who hear the calling to pass down their knowledge and love of jazz music to the next generation.

We also have a numuber of ways that non-musicians can also get involved helping out so please do not hesitate to reach out and ask how you could help, even if you don’t play an instrument!

## Student Mentor Volunteer

Our student mentor volunteer program is a new exciting way for mentors to get involved outside of our studio space. Through an application program a select number of students can apply to have a personal mentor. We will match up our mentors with students who play the same instrument. This program can greatly impact students with one-on-one guidance. We are working on a virtual program for this as well so stay tuned!"

"<webfetch-citation source_id="UbhHMFxJJOE5oWoLqE-mv" />

## In-School Programs

## We bring the JAZZ to your school!

For years now the Jazz Angels have been partnering with schools around the Long Beach and Great Los Angeles Area. If you are a school administrator or band teacher let the Angels come to your school and work their magic with the kids. Barry and team have been working through various grant projects to bring customized jazz programs to both middle school’s and high school’s around LA. We will come visit your school and get a feel for the demographics and will customize the music arrangements for the best possible results. Programs with schools can be customized with the Angels coming to teach jazz once a month, bi-weekly, or weekly depending on the school’s needs.

Our in-school programs in the Paramount Unified School District reach over 200 middle school music students in a once-weekly jazz lesson as part of their regular music class instruction.

This gives the students additional exposure to improvisational jazz while allowing their regular music teacher the freedom to circulate around the room to observe and give individual attention to students as needed. We welcome participation from all local schools/districts who are interested."

## Our Story

## How Jazz Angels came to be

In 2007 Barry Cogert and Albert Alva founded Jazz Angels. Both Barry and Albert are professional musicians and teachers and recognized a need for a unique type of music education for the youth. They wanted to make jazz accessible to the youth of Southern California – so they started the Jazz Angels non-profit with the goal of keeping jazz alive and strong for future generations.

Still today, the purpose is to create an opportunity for young people to hear and learn about jazz, encourage them to play an instrument, and provide opportunities for them to perform. Much more than music lessons in Long Beach, this is a program that provides a rich life experience for a young musician. A unique component to The Jazz Angels methodology is to engage professional musicians as mentors who play alongside the participants, providing inspiration and instilling pride and discipline.

## Why Jazz Angels?

The Jazz Angels is a non-profit public benefit corporation, registered under IRS code 501(c)(3). Programs include both in-school supplemental music instruction and ensemble-based jazz instruction at our studio in Signal Hill, CA. **Federal Tax Exempt ID number: 84-1686341**"

Programs include in-school performances, masterclasses, and jazz combo instruction at schools—introducing students to the history, creativity, and cultural significance of jazz. We also offer special youth concerts like *Jazz Day for Kids* and partner with local schools and organizations to reach those who may not otherwise have access to music education.

In addition, our monthly *Jazz Jams* provide a welcoming and inclusive space for musicians of all ages and skill levels to collaborate, improvise, and build community through music making.
